lessons.L01:
# Automating Quality: ruff, mypy, and pytest

**Module**: M01 · The Python Quality Toolchain  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claims**: C1, C11 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

The quality foundation for any production Python script is three tools: **ruff** (style and lint), **mypy** (type enforcement), and **pytest** (behaviour verification). A script is not production-ready until all three pass. This is not a preference — it is the convergence point of 22 official sources including PEP 8, the Google Style Guide, and the mypy documentation.

Each tool fills a gap the others cannot. ruff enforces formatting and lint rules that make code readable and catches obvious bugs ([S017](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/)). mypy catches type mismatches that only reveal themselves at runtime under edge cases — things ruff cannot see ([S011](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)). pytest verifies that the script actually does what it claims ([S006](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/explanation/goodpractices.html)). None of them substitute for the others.

## Why it matters when teaching

When a developer asks "do I really need all three?", the answer lies in the failure modes each one prevents. Without ruff, style inconsistencies accumulate until code review becomes a style argument instead of a logic review. Without mypy, functions accept wrong types silently until a production edge case surfaces the bug. Without pytest, the script "works on my machine" until it doesn't.

The meta-point to communicate: enforcement by toolchain is more reliable than enforcement by discipline. A rule in a config file never gets tired, never rushes a review, and never makes exceptions for senior developers.

## A concrete example

Here is the minimal `pyproject.toml` block that activates all three:

```toml
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88
select = ["E", "F", "W", "I", "D", "UP", "B"]
fix = true

[tool.ruff.lint.pydocstyle]
convention = "google"

[tool.mypy]
strict = true
python_version = "3.10"

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
addopts = "--strict-markers -q"
```

One file, three tools configured. When a developer adds a function missing a return type annotation, `mypy --strict` fails. When they use `Optional[str]` instead of `str | None`, ruff's `UP007` rule auto-fixes it. When they break a behaviour, pytest catches it.

## Key points

- ruff replaces five tools (flake8, black, isort, pydocstyle, pyupgrade) — configure it once, not five times
- mypy `strict = true` is the correct setting for new projects; use incremental mode only for existing unannotated codebases
- All three belong in `pyproject.toml` — one file, one place to look

## Go deeper

- [S017 — Ruff docs](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) — full rule reference and `--fix` semantics
- [S011 — mypy docs](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) — `--strict` flag breakdown and incremental adoption path

---

*[Next lesson: pyproject.toml — One File to Configure Them All →](./L02-pyproject-toml.md)*

# pyproject.toml: One File to Configure Them All

**Module**: M01 · The Python Quality Toolchain  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claim**: C2 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

`pyproject.toml` is the single configuration file for a Python script project. It holds dev tool configuration (`[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.mypy]`, `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`) and, via the `tomllib` stdlib module (Python 3.11+), can also serve as the runtime configuration source for the script itself.

Four independent tools — pytest ([S006](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/explanation/goodpractices.html)), mypy ([S011](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)), ruff ([S017](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/)), and tomllib ([S009](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tomllib.html)) — each independently document `pyproject.toml` as their configuration home. This convergence is now the de facto standard for any Python project created since 2022.

## Why it matters when teaching

The most common objection is "we already have a `setup.cfg` / `tox.ini` / `.flake8` / `mypy.ini`". The answer: that is four files, any of which can be inconsistent with the others. `pyproject.toml` eliminates the question "which config applies here?" by making the answer always the same file.

The second teaching point is the dual role. `tomllib` (read-only, stdlib since 3.11) means the script can read its own application configuration from a TOML file at runtime — the same format used by the dev tools. Developers who work in one format become fluent in both uses faster.

## A concrete example

**Dev tool config** (tool chain is configured here, not in separate files):

```toml
# pyproject.toml
[tool.ruff]
line-length = 88

[tool.mypy]
strict = true

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
```

**Runtime app config** (the script reads its own settings from TOML at startup):

```python
import tomllib
from pathlib import Path

def load_config(config_path: Path) -> dict[str, object]:
    with config_path.open("rb") as f:
        return tomllib.load(f)
```

The script can read from `pyproject.toml` directly using a `[tool.myscript]` section, or from a separate `config.toml` supplied by the operator. Either way, the format is identical: typed (strings, ints, booleans, arrays natively), readable, and non-executable (unlike Python config files).

**The one caveat to teach**: tomllib is read-only. If the script needs to write config back to disk, use `tomli_w` (third-party) or a separate format for write-back.

## Key points

- `pyproject.toml` is the primary config artifact — configure every dev tool here, not in separate files
- `tomllib` (Python 3.11+) makes TOML a first-class runtime config format; use `tomli` as a backport for 3.10 targets
- For end-user-facing config that non-technical users edit by hand, INI (configparser) is still valid — TOML is for developer-facing configuration

## Go deeper

- [S009 — tomllib docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tomllib.html) — module API and TOML syntax reference
- [S008 — configparser docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html) — when INI is still appropriate

---

*[← L01: Automating Quality](./L01-quality-toolchain.md)* · *[L03: The Universal Script Skeleton →](./L03-script-skeleton.md)*
lessons.L03:
# The Universal Script Skeleton

**Module**: M02 · Script Structure and Observability  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claim**: C5 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Every Python script should follow a fixed structural pattern: define a `main()` function that executes in a specific sequence, and call it from an `if __name__ == "__main__"` guard at the bottom of the file. This is not style preference — the Google Style Guide ([S003](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)), the argparse docs ([S005](https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html)), the logging HOWTO ([S004](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html)), and the asyncio docs ([S012](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html)) all independently converge on this structure.

The execution sequence inside `main()` must follow a fixed order:
1. Parse arguments (argparse)
2. Configure logging (`basicConfig()`)
3. Validate inputs
4. Dispatch to domain functions

This order is not arbitrary. Logging must be configured *before* domain functions run so their log output is correctly routed. Arguments must be parsed before logging configuration so the `--log-level` flag can be applied.

## Why it matters when teaching

The `if __name__ == "__main__"` guard is widely known but rarely explained. The payoff: it makes the script *importable* in tests without triggering execution. Without it, `import myscript` in a test file runs the entire script. With it, tests can import and call individual functions in isolation.

The fixed execution sequence matters for a different reason: it creates a predictable mental model. Anyone reading an unfamiliar script knows exactly where to look for CLI behaviour, where to look for log configuration, and where the "real work" starts. This is leverage when teaching onboarding — every script in the codebase is structured the same way.

## A concrete example

```python
import argparse
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def process(data: str) -> str:
    logger.debug("Processing: %s", data)
    return data.upper()


def parse_args() -> argparse.Namespace:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="My script")
    parser.add_argument("data", help="Input string to process")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--log-level",
        default="WARNING",
        choices=["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR"],
    )
    return parser.parse_args()


def main() -> None:
    args = parse_args()                          # 1. parse
    logging.basicConfig(level=args.log_level)    # 2. configure logging
    result = process(args.data)                  # 3+4. validate + dispatch
    print(result)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

For async scripts, only the entry point changes — the sequence is identical:

```python
import asyncio

async def main() -> None:
    ...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

## Key points

- `if __name__ == "__main__": main()` enables test imports with zero side effects
- The execution order is fixed: parse → configure logging → validate → dispatch
- All domain logic lives in importable functions, never at module level

## Go deeper

- [S003 — Google Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html) — the `main()` pattern and entry guard requirement
- [S005 — argparse docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html) — argument definition, subparsers, and env var defaults

---

*[← L02: pyproject.toml](./L02-pyproject-toml.md)* · *[L04: Structured Logging →](./L04-structured-logging.md)*

# Structured Logging Over print()

**Module**: M02 · Script Structure and Observability  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 10 minutes  
**Claim**: C4 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Replace every `print()` call used for status or diagnostic output with the standard `logging` module. The pattern has three rules:

1. **One `getLogger` per module**, at module level, named `__name__`
2. **One `basicConfig()` call**, in `main()`, applied once at startup
3. **Level controlled at runtime** via a `--log-level` CLI argument, not by editing source code

This pattern is documented in the Python logging HOWTO ([S004](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html)) and is the baseline across every professional Python codebase.

## Why it matters when teaching

`print()` is a debugger that shipped to production. It cannot be silenced without deleting it. It cannot be redirected to a file. It cannot carry severity levels. It produces no timestamps or source location. When you teach someone to reach for `logger.info()` instead of `print("Starting...")`, you are teaching them to write observable code — code that can be monitored, filtered, and retained without modification.

There is one important distinction to teach clearly: **scripts and libraries follow different rules**. Scripts configure logging (they are the application). Libraries must never configure logging — they emit log records and install a `NullHandler`. This prevents silent log pollution when a library is imported into a larger application.

```python
# ✅ Correct: in a library module
import logging
logging.getLogger(__name__).addHandler(logging.NullHandler())
```

## A concrete example

Complete logging setup for a script:

```python
import argparse
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)   # module-level, named __name__


def fetch_data(url: str) -> bytes:
    logger.info("Fetching %s", url)
    # ...
    return b""


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("url")
    parser.add_argument("--log-level", default="WARNING")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logging.basicConfig(            # one call, in main(), never elsewhere
        level=args.log_level,
        format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s — %(message)s",
    )

    data = fetch_data(args.url)
    logger.debug("Got %d bytes", len(data))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Run with `python fetch.py https://example.com --log-level DEBUG` to see all output. Without `--log-level`, only `WARNING` and above appears. No source edits required.

## Key points

- `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` at module level provides automatic source location in logs
- Call `logging.basicConfig()` exactly once, in `main()`, never inside imported library code
- Export a `--log-level` argument so callers can change verbosity without touching source code

## Go deeper

- [S004 — Python logging HOWTO](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html) — `NullHandler` pattern, structlog integration, and handler configuration
- [S003 — Google Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html) — the library/script distinction stated explicitly

---

*[← L03: Script Skeleton](./L03-script-skeleton.md)* · *[L05: Self-Documenting Functions →](./L05-self-documenting-functions.md)*

# Writing Self-Documenting Functions

**Module**: M03 · Documentation and the Type System  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claim**: C3 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Every public function must have three things: a one-line summary docstring, type annotations on all parameters and return values, and raised-exception documentation when non-obvious. This combination — mandated by Google Style Guide ([S003](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)), PEP 257 ([S002](https://peps.python.org/pep-0257/)), and enforced by ruff's pydocstyle plugin ([S017](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/)) — makes functions legible without running them.

The critical word is *public*. Functions starting with `_` are internal; heavy documentation is optional. Functions that form the interface of a module — anything a caller might import — must be fully documented.

## Why it matters when teaching

When teaching this, two objections come up repeatedly. First: *"Type annotations are redundant if I have docstrings."* They are not. Docstrings describe intent in prose. Type annotations are machine-verifiable contracts. Mypy will catch `process("hello")` being called where `process(42)` is expected. No docstring does that.

Second: *"I'll add docs when the function is stable."* This is documentation debt. The function is hardest to document when it was just written, because the details are still fluid. But the *most valuable time* to document it is also right then — before you forget why the design is what it is. Teach learners to write the docstring first, before the implementation: it forces them to clarify what the function does before they write how.

## A concrete example

Google-style docstring with type annotations (the standard for Python professional codebases):

```python
def extract_urls(text: str, *, scheme: str = "https") -> list[str]:
    """Extract all URLs with the given scheme from a block of text.

    Args:
        text: Raw input text, may include multiple lines.
        scheme: URL scheme to filter by (e.g. "https", "ftp").
            Defaults to "https".

    Returns:
        A list of URL strings found in the text, preserving order.
        Returns an empty list if no URLs are found.

    Raises:
        ValueError: If ``scheme`` is an empty string.
    """
    if not scheme:
        raise ValueError("scheme must not be empty")
    # implementation
    return []
```

Notice what this function communicates without running it: what it accepts, what it returns on the happy path, what it returns on the empty case, and what it raises and why. A reader — including a future AI tool — can reason about this function completely from its signature and docstring.

Ruff enforces the docstring rule automatically:

```toml
[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["D"]   # pydocstyle checks — missing docstring = lint error
```

## Key points

- Write the docstring before the implementation — it clarifies the contract and prevents documentation debt
- `Args`, `Returns`, `Raises` sections are mandatory for non-trivial public functions
- Type annotations are machine-verifiable; docstrings are human-readable — both serve different purposes and neither replaces the other

## Go deeper

- [S002 — PEP 257](https://peps.python.org/pep-0257/) — canonical docstring conventions, one-line vs multi-line format
- [S003 — Google Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html) — Args/Returns/Raises structure and enforcement examples

---

*[← L04: Structured Logging](./L04-structured-logging.md)* · *[L06: The Type System →](./L06-type-system.md)*

# Python 3.10+ Type Annotations in Practice

**Module**: M03 · Documentation and the Type System  
**Type**: applied  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claim**: C10 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Python 3.10 simplified type annotation syntax significantly: use `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, `list[str]` instead of `List[str]`, and `dict[str, int]` instead of `Dict[str, int]`. The old forms still work — mypy ([S011](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)) and Python 3.10+ both accept them — but new code should use the modern forms. They are shorter, more readable, and require no imports from `typing`.

Beyond the syntax changes, four constructs solve real problems that simpler annotations cannot: `TypedDict` for typed dictionary structures, `Protocol` for structural typing, `Final` for constants, and `TypeAlias` for readable complex types.

## Why it matters when teaching

The value of the modern type system is not catching bugs you already know about — it is catching bugs you didn't know were possible. When you annotate a function as returning `str | None`, mypy will flag every call site that treats the result as a plain `str` without checking for `None`. That is a whole class of `AttributeError` and `TypeError` eliminated before the code runs.

When teaching, the comparison between old and new syntax is a useful anchor. Then the four advanced constructs give intermediate learners an upgrade path: they know how to annotate scalar values, but they may not know how to annotate a configuration dictionary or a class-independent interface.

## A concrete example

**Old vs. new syntax** (Python 3.10+):

```python
# Old (still valid, avoid in new code)
from typing import Optional, List, Dict
def process(items: List[str], limit: Optional[int] = None) -> Dict[str, int]: ...

# New (prefer this)
def process(items: list[str], limit: int | None = None) -> dict[str, int]: ...
```

**TypedDict** — for dictionaries with known shapes:

```python
from typing import TypedDict

class Config(TypedDict):
    host: str
    port: int
    debug: bool

def connect(cfg: Config) -> None:
    ...  # mypy enforces that cfg["host"] exists and is str
```

**Protocol** — structural typing without inheritance:

```python
from typing import Protocol

class Closeable(Protocol):
    def close(self) -> None: ...

def shutdown(resource: Closeable) -> None:
    resource.close()   # works for any class with close(), no inheritance required
```

**Final** — runtime-visible constants:

```python
from typing import Final

MAX_RETRIES: Final = 3   # mypy prevents reassignment
```

Run `mypy --strict` to enforce these across an entire project:

```toml
[tool.mypy]
strict = true
```

## Key points

- Prefer `X | None` over `Optional[X]` and `list[X]` over `List[X]` in Python 3.10+ code
- Use `TypedDict` to give typed shapes to config dictionaries instead of `dict[str, Any]`
- `Protocol` enables structural typing — write interfaces without requiring inheritance

## Go deeper

- [S011 — mypy documentation](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) — `--strict` mode checks, `TypedDict` and `Protocol` support
- [S010 — PEP 604](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html) — the `X | Y` union syntax specification and rationale

---

*[← L05: Self-Documenting Functions](./L05-self-documenting-functions.md)* · *[L07: pathlib and Context Managers →](./L07-pathlib-context-managers.md)*

# pathlib and Context Managers

**Module**: M04 · Resource Management and Configuration  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claims**: C7, C8 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Two practices eliminate entire categories of resource bugs. First: use `pathlib.Path` for every file system operation — never `os.path`. Second: use `with` statements (context managers) for every resource that must be released — files, network connections, temporary directories, thread locks.

Both are supported across Python 3.8+ and documented in the official stdlib ([S007](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html), [S001](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/)). They are not advanced features — they are the baseline.

## Why it matters when teaching

`os.path` is string concatenation with delimiters. It works, but it fails silently on cross-platform paths, offers no autocompletion on path components, and spreads path logic across multiple modules. `pathlib.Path` makes paths first-class objects: methods like `.read_text()`, `.write_text()`, `.glob()`, `.parent`, and the `/` operator for joining make path intent explicit and eliminate a class of string-handling bugs.

Context managers address a different problem: resources that must be closed even when exceptions occur. The pattern `with open(path) as f:` is familiar — but the same contract applies to database connections, HTTP sessions, temporary directories, and asyncio locks. Without `with`, a crash before `.close()` leaks the resource. With `with`, the `__exit__` method is guaranteed to run.

The comparison that lands best in teaching: `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` vs `tempfile.mktemp()`. Both create temporary directories. But `mktemp()` does not clean up — and between creating the name and creating the directory, another process could create the same path (a race condition). `TemporaryDirectory` as a context manager creates, provides, and removes the directory atomically.

## A concrete example

```python
from pathlib import Path
import tempfile

def process_config_files(config_dir: Path) -> list[str]:
    """Read all .toml files from a directory and return their contents."""
    results = []
    for path in config_dir.glob("*.toml"):          # Path.glob — no os.listdir
        content = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")  # Path.read_text — no open()
        results.append(content)
    return results


def write_report(output_dir: Path, name: str, body: str) -> Path:
    """Write a report file, creating parent directories if needed."""
    output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    report_path = output_dir / name                 # / operator for joining
    report_path.write_text(body, encoding="utf-8")
    return report_path


def build_in_tempdir(source: Path) -> str:
    """Process files in a guaranteed-cleanup temporary directory."""
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:      # always cleaned up
        tmp_path = Path(tmp)
        (tmp_path / "work.txt").write_text("data")
        return (tmp_path / "work.txt").read_text()
    # tmp_path is deleted here, even if an exception was raised
```

## Key points

- `pathlib.Path` replaces `os.path`, `os.getcwd()`, `open()`, and string-based path joins — prefer it for all filesystem operations
- The `with` statement guarantees `__exit__` runs even when exceptions occur — use it for every resource that must be released
- `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory()` as a context manager eliminates cleanup bugs and `mktemp()` race conditions

## Go deeper

- [S007 — pathlib documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html) — full method reference, `Path.stat()`, `Path.rename()`, `Path.unlink()`
- [S001 — contextlib documentation](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/) — `contextlib.contextmanager` decorator for building custom context managers

---

# Configuration and Secrets

**Module**: M04 · Resource Management and Configuration  
**Type**: applied  
**Estimated time**: 11 minutes  
**Claim**: C6 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Configuration must never live in source code. Instead, follow a four-tier priority stack — highest to lowest — applied at runtime:

1. **CLI flags** — `argparse` arguments when the user needs per-run control
2. **Environment variables** — `os.environ.get()` for deployment-time values (especially secrets)
3. **Config files** — TOML (`tomllib`, stdlib since 3.11) or INI (`configparser`, stdlib) for persistent settings
4. **Hardcoded defaults** — the minimum safe value assumed when nothing else is set

This priority order is the consensus recommendation from the Real Python guide ([S003](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)), the argparse docs ([S005](https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html)), and the tomllib addition to the standard library ([S009](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tomllib.html)).

Secrets are environment variables, never files committed to version control.

## Why it matters when teaching

The most common configuration mistake in scripts is reading a `.env` file and committing it. The second most common is embedding an API key in source code and then rotating it manually after an incident. The four-tier stack is not complex — it is a decision rule that answers "where should this value come from?" for every configuration need.

`tomllib` is the right choice for structured developer configuration: it is read-only (prevents accidental writes), in the standard library since 3.11, and available as `tomli` for 3.8–3.10. `configparser` remains viable for end-user-facing configuration where plain INI files are more approachable.

## A concrete example

```python
import argparse
import logging
import os
import tomllib
from pathlib import Path

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

DEFAULT_CONFIG = {"host": "localhost", "port": 8080, "workers": 2}


def load_file_config(path: Path) -> dict[str, object]:
    """Load TOML config file; return empty dict if file absent."""
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    with path.open("rb") as f:
        return tomllib.load(f)   # read-only by design: tomllib has no dump()


def resolve_config(args: argparse.Namespace) -> dict[str, object]:
    """Merge config from all sources, highest-priority last (wins)."""
    cfg: dict[str, object] = dict(DEFAULT_CONFIG)

    # Tier 3: config file
    file_cfg = load_file_config(Path("config.toml"))
    cfg.update(file_cfg)

    # Tier 2: environment variables
    if host := os.environ.get("APP_HOST"):
        cfg["host"] = host
    if secret := os.environ.get("APP_SECRET"):
        cfg["secret"] = secret          # secrets never in files
    else:
        logger.warning("APP_SECRET not set — features may be limited")

    # Tier 1: CLI (applied last — highest priority)
    if args.host:
        cfg["host"] = args.host

    return cfg
```

Notice: `APP_SECRET` is read from the environment only. There is no TOML key for secrets — if the secret ends up in a file, it is one `git add` away from a leak.

## Key points

- CLI > env vars > config file > hardcoded default: apply them in this order, highest-priority last (so it overwrites)
- Secrets belong in environment variables only — never in config files or source code
- `tomllib` is read-only by design: it parses TOML but cannot write it, preventing accidental config corruption

## Go deeper

- [S009 — tomllib](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tomllib.html) — standard library since 3.11, `tomli` backport instructions, binary file requirement
- [S008 — configparser](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html) — INI-format config, interpolation, fallback keys

---

*[← L07: pathlib and Context Managers](./L07-pathlib-context-managers.md)* · *[L09: Serialization →](./L09-serialization.md)*

# Choosing the Right Serialization Format

**Module**: M05 · Data Serialization  
**Type**: core  
**Estimated time**: 12 minutes  
**Claim**: C9 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Serialization format choice is a security and compatibility decision, not just a preference. The rule is based on trust level and edit context:

| Format | Use when | Trust required | Standard library |
|--------|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| JSON | External data exchange, APIs, logs | Untrusted input OK | `json` |
| CSV | Tabular data, spreadsheet export | Untrusted input OK | `csv` |
| TOML | Developer and tool configuration | Trusted authors | `tomllib` (3.11+) |
| INI | End-user editable config | Trusted authors | `configparser` |
| pickle | Internal caching, IPC between trusted processes | Trusted processes only | `pickle` |

The Python docs ([S021](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html)), Real Python ([S014](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html)) and the TOML spec ([S015](https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html)) all address this — and the one rule they agree on is: **never deserialize pickle data received over a network or from an unknown file.**

## Why it matters when teaching

`pickle` is the format most likely to cause a security incident in a script. It executes arbitrary Python code on deserialization. A script that caches to pickle and then loads that file later is safe when the file is written and read by the same process on the same machine. But the moment the file is transmitted, shared, or received from a network endpoint, it becomes a remote code execution vector.

The comparison that makes this concrete: `json.loads(data)` on malicious input raises `json.JSONDecodeError`. `pickle.loads(data)` on malicious input executes the attacker's code.

For teaching, the format table is the anchor. Ask learners to categorize their last three serialization choices by that table. Most will find at least one case where they reached for pickle or JSON out of habit rather than reasoning about trust level.

## A concrete example

```python
import json
import pickle
from pathlib import Path


# ✅ JSON for data that may come from outside your process
def save_results(results: list[dict[str, str]], path: Path) -> None:
    path.write_text(json.dumps(results, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")


def load_results(path: Path) -> list[dict[str, str]]:
    return json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))


# ⚠️  pickle for internal caching only — never load from untrusted source
def cache_model(model: object, path: Path) -> None:
    """Cache a trained model locally. Load only from paths you created."""
    with path.open("wb") as f:
        pickle.dump(model, f)


def load_model_cache(path: Path) -> object:
    # Only safe if path was written by this process on this machine
    with path.open("rb") as f:
        return pickle.load(f)  # noqa: S301 — intentional, internal cache only
```

The `# noqa: S301` comment is intentional: ruff's security rules will flag `pickle.load`. Suppress it explicitly with a comment explaining why the use is safe, rather than disabling the rule project-wide.

## Key points

- JSON for external or network data; pickle for trusted-only internal caching — never transmit pickle over a network
- The rule is based on trust level, not file format familiarity
- When suppressing a security linter warning, do it on one line with an explanation — never disable the check project-wide

## Go deeper

- [S021 — pickle security](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html) — the official warning: "Never unpickle data that could have come from an untrusted source"
- [S014 — JSON best practices](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html) — datetime serialization, custom encoders, `json.JSONDecodeError` handling

---

*[← L08: Configuration and Secrets](./L08-configuration-secrets.md)* · *[L10: Concurrency Decision Matrix →](./L10-concurrency-decision-matrix.md)*

# asyncio, Threads, or Processes: Decision Matrix

**Module**: M06 · Concurrency and Graceful Shutdown  
**Type**: applied  
**Estimated time**: 13 minutes  
**Claim**: C12 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Python offers three concurrency models, and the choice is determined by the bottleneck — not preference:

| Workload | Model | Reason |
|----------|-------|--------|
| I/O-bound (network, disk) | `asyncio` or `ThreadPoolExecutor` | GIL released during I/O waits |
| CPU-bound (heavy computation) | `ProcessPoolExecutor` | Bypasses the GIL by spawning processes |
| Mixing sync and async | `asyncio.to_thread()` | Runs blocking calls off the event loop |
| Fan-out of concurrent tasks | `asyncio.TaskGroup` (3.11+) | Structured concurrency with automatic cancellation |

This matrix is the consensus from the Python asyncio docs ([S012](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html)), the concurrent.futures docs ([S013](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html)), and Real Python ([S019](https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html)).

## Why it matters when teaching

The most common mistake is using `asyncio` for CPU-bound work. `asyncio` is cooperative — tasks yield control voluntarily when doing I/O. A CPU-bound coroutine that never yields will block the entire event loop for every other coroutine. The fix is not "use asyncio better" — it is `ProcessPoolExecutor`, combined with `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor()` to offload the blocking work.

The second most common mistake is over-threading I/O-bound work. Threads solve the problem (GIL is released during I/O), but `asyncio` can handle thousands of concurrent I/O operations on a single thread, while thousands of threads consume gigabytes of stack space.

When teaching the decision matrix, ask: "Is the program waiting or computing?" That one question eliminates most wrong choices.

## A concrete example

**asyncio for I/O-bound work:**

```python
import asyncio
import httpx


async def fetch(client: httpx.AsyncClient, url: str) -> str:
    response = await client.get(url)
    return response.text


async def fetch_all(urls: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:   # 3.11+ — automatic cancellation on error
            tasks = [tg.create_task(fetch(client, url)) for url in urls]
    return [t.result() for t in tasks]
```

**ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-bound work:**

```python
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor


def crunch(n: int) -> int:
    return sum(i * i for i in range(n))  # CPU-bound


def run_parallel(values: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:   # context manager — waits for all workers
        return list(pool.map(crunch, values))
```

**asyncio.to_thread for mixing sync libraries into async code:**

```python
import asyncio


async def save_to_disk(data: bytes, path: str) -> None:
    # blocking write moved off event loop
    await asyncio.to_thread(open(path, "wb").write, data)
```

## Key points

- I/O-bound → asyncio (thousands of concurrent waits, single thread); CPU-bound → ProcessPoolExecutor (bypasses GIL)
- `asyncio.TaskGroup` (3.11+) is the preferred way to fan out concurrent tasks — it cancels all siblings if one fails
- Use `asyncio.to_thread()` to call synchronous library code from an async function without blocking the event loop

## Go deeper

- [S012 — asyncio docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html) — TaskGroup, `run_in_executor`, event loop lifecycle
- [S013 — concurrent.futures](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html) — `ThreadPoolExecutor` vs `ProcessPoolExecutor`, `as_completed`, timeout handling

---

*[← L09: Serialization](./L09-serialization.md)* · *[L11: Graceful Shutdown →](./L11-graceful-shutdown.md)*

# Graceful Shutdown: SIGTERM, CancelledError, and Cleanup

**Module**: M06 · Concurrency and Graceful Shutdown  
**Type**: applied  
**Estimated time**: 13 minutes  
**Claim**: C13 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Scripts that run in production — in containers, under systemd, or as background workers — receive shutdown signals. A script that ignores `SIGTERM` will be forcibly killed after a timeout, losing in-flight work. Graceful shutdown means: handle the signal, cancel running tasks, and run cleanup code before exiting.

Three components work together ([S016](https://docs.python.org/3/library/signal.html), [S013](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html), [S019](https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html)):

1. **Signal handler** — registers callback for `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT`
2. **CancelledError + finally** — async tasks clean up resources when cancelled
3. **Executor context managers** — `with ProcessPoolExecutor()` drains queued work before exiting

## Why it matters when teaching

Graceful shutdown is a topic most script authors encounter only after their first production incident: a containerized script killed mid-transaction, leaving corrupted state. The pattern must be taught before that incident, not learned from it.

The `signal` module is the right tool — not `atexit`, which runs only on normal interpreter exit and not on all platforms for `SIGTERM`. On Windows, `SIGTERM` is not a real signal — it is emulated, and `SIGINT` (`Ctrl+C`) is the primary keyboard interrupt. Teach this caveat explicitly so learners cross-platform code correctly.

The mental model: `finally` blocks in `async` functions run even when the coroutine is cancelled. That is where database commits, file flushes, and connection closes belong.

## A concrete example

```python
import asyncio
import logging
import signal
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


async def worker(name: str) -> None:
    """A long-running async task that handles cancellation cleanly."""
    try:
        logger.info("Worker %s starting", name)
        while True:
            await asyncio.sleep(1)    # yields to event loop — cancellable here
            logger.debug("Worker %s tick", name)
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        logger.info("Worker %s received cancel — running cleanup", name)
        raise   # always re-raise CancelledError so the event loop knows
    finally:
        logger.info("Worker %s cleaned up", name)   # always runs


async def main() -> None:
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    shutdown_event = asyncio.Event()

    def handle_signal(sig: signal.Signals) -> None:
        logger.warning("Received %s — initiating shutdown", sig.name)
        shutdown_event.set()

    # Register signals (SIGTERM from container orchestrators, SIGINT from Ctrl+C)
    loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, handle_signal, signal.SIGTERM)
    loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, handle_signal, signal.SIGINT)

    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        task = tg.create_task(worker("main"))
        await shutdown_event.wait()   # block until signal received
        task.cancel()                 # triggers CancelledError in worker


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # ProcessPoolExecutor as context manager drains queued CPU tasks on exit
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
        asyncio.run(main())
```

> **Windows caveat**: `loop.add_signal_handler()` is not available on Windows. Use `signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, handler)` and handle only keyboard interrupt. `SIGTERM` cannot be registered on Windows — rely on platform process management instead.

## Key points

- Register `SIGTERM` and `SIGINT` handlers early in `main()` to respond to container orchestrator signals
- Always re-raise `CancelledError` in an `except` block — catching and swallowing it breaks cooperative cancellation
- `with ProcessPoolExecutor()` and `with ThreadPoolExecutor()` wait for queued tasks to complete on `__exit__`

## Go deeper

- [S016 — signal module](https://docs.python.org/3/library/signal.html) — `loop.add_signal_handler`, platform differences, `asyncio.Event` vs threading.Event
- [S019 — asyncio shutdown patterns](https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html) — task cancellation lifecycle, `TaskGroup` cancellation semantics

---

*[← L10: Concurrency Decision Matrix](./L10-concurrency-decision-matrix.md)* · *[L12: The Type Annotation Debate →](./L12-type-annotation-debate.md)*

# The Type Annotation Debate

**Module**: M07 · Teaching Complications and Honest Limits  
**Type**: debate  
**Estimated time**: 10 minutes  
**Claim**: C3 (debate framing) from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

This lesson presents a genuine disagreement in the Python community — not to resolve it, but to prepare you to teach it honestly.

**PEP 484 (2015)**, which introduced type annotations, states explicitly: *"It should also be emphasized that Python will remain a dynamically typed language, and the authors have no desire to ever make type hints mandatory or even ubiquitous."*

**Google's Python Style Guide** ([S003](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)) says: *"Be consistent with existing code. For new code, annotate all functions."*

Both documents are authoritative. They occupy different positions on the same axis: PEP 484 describes what the language permits; Google Style describes what a production team should require.

## The historical context

PEP 484 was adopted in 2015 when type annotations were new and editor tooling was immature. The authors were cautious: they wanted adoption without mandating a paradigm shift. At the time, mypy was a research project, and most Python codebases had no annotations at all.

By 2024, the ecosystem has matured. Mypy, pyright, and ruff are stable and widely adopted. IDEs infer types from annotations in real time. Modern Python style guides — including Google's and the Python Packaging Authority's — recommend annotations for all public API surfaces. The practical consensus has shifted without PEP 484 being revised.

## Why this matters when teaching

Students who read PEP 484 will quote the "never mandatory" language and argue against annotation requirements. If you teach type annotations as an unambiguous rule, you will be contradicted by a primary source.

The honest answer: **PEP 484 describes language policy; professional style guides describe team practice.** These are not in conflict — they operate at different levels of authority. For new projects, full annotation is the professional default. For existing codebases, incremental adoption (annotating as you touch files) is the pragmatic approach.

The takeaway for students: When joining a codebase, match its annotation discipline. When starting a project, use `mypy --strict` from day one. Never quote PEP 484's "never mandatory" as a reason to skip annotations in production code.

## No code example

This lesson does not include a code example — the debate is about policy, not syntax. The relevant syntax is covered in [L06: The Type System](./L06-type-system.md).

## Key points

- PEP 484's "never mandatory" describes language design policy, not professional coding practice — the two are not in conflict
- The professional consensus in 2024 is full annotation for new public code; incremental annotation for migrations
- When students cite PEP 484 to avoid annotations, the resolution is: teach the distinction between language policy and team standards

## Go deeper

- [S022 — PEP 484](https://peps.python.org/pep-0484/) — the original "never mandatory" language in context
- [S011 — mypy documentation](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) — `--strict` mode, migration strategies

---

*[← L11: Graceful Shutdown](./L11-graceful-shutdown.md)* · *[L13: Gaps and Follow-Ups →](./L13-gaps-and-followups.md)*

# Gaps and Follow-Ups: What This Course Does Not Cover

**Module**: M07 · Teaching Complications and Honest Limits  
**Type**: gap  
**Estimated time**: 8 minutes  
**Source**: gaps.md from Strata research (G010–G013)

---

## The core idea

This course is built from 22 sources spanning the most commonly cited Python scripting best practices. Four important topics fell outside that scope — not because they are unimportant, but because the research base was insufficient to teach them to the same standard as the other 13 lessons. Naming the gaps is part of honest curriculum design.

## The four gaps

### G010 — Packaging and Distribution

This course covers scripts: single-file programs designed to be run directly. It does not cover how to package those scripts as installable Python packages (e.g., publishing to PyPI, building wheels, defining `[project.scripts]` entry points in `pyproject.toml`).

**Where to go next**: [Python Packaging User Guide](https://packaging.python.org/) — the authoritative reference from the Python Packaging Authority.

---

### G011 — Dependency Management

This course uses `pyproject.toml` for tool configuration, but does not address how to manage runtime dependencies: virtual environments, pinning, `uv`, Poetry, or pip-compile workflows.

**Where to go next**: [uv documentation](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) — the fastest modern Python dependency manager, from the same team that built ruff. Also: [pip-tools](https://pip-tools.readthedocs.io/) for lock-file generation without virtual environment management.

---

### G012 — Pre-commit Hooks

This course teaches `ruff` and `mypy` as tools you run manually or in CI. It does not cover `pre-commit` — the framework that runs these checks automatically before each `git commit`, preventing lint failures from reaching CI.

**Where to go next**: [pre-commit documentation](https://pre-commit.com/) — setup takes under 10 minutes. A recommended config for this course's toolchain:

```yaml
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
    rev: v0.4.0
    hooks:
      - id: ruff
      - id: ruff-format
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-mypy
    rev: v1.9.0
    hooks:
      - id: mypy
```

---

### G013 — Dataclasses and attrs

The course uses `TypedDict` for typed dictionary structures, but does not cover `dataclasses` (stdlib since 3.7) or the `attrs` library for defining typed data containers as classes. These are significant patterns in production Python scripting.

**Where to go next**: [dataclasses documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html) — start here. [attrs documentation](https://www.attrs.org/) for larger projects needing validators and converters.

---

## Why this matters when teaching

Acknowledging gaps signals intellectual honesty and gives learners a roadmap beyond this course. It prevents the anti-pattern of presenting a partial toolkit as a complete one.

Every curriculum is a set of choices about what to include and what to defer. The right response to a gap is not to fill it with shallowly-researched content — it is to name it, state the confidence level, and point to the best available next resource.

## Key points

- Packaging, dependency management, pre-commit hooks, and dataclasses are the four topics this course intentionally defers
- Each gap has a specific "go next" resource — learners are not left without direction
- Teaching honest curriculum limits builds credibility with intermediate-level learners who will notice the gaps regardless

---

*[← L12: The Type Annotation Debate](./L12-type-annotation-debate.md)*

cards.front: What is ruff, and what tools does it replace?
cards.back: Ruff is a fast Python linter and formatter written in Rust. It replaces flake8, isort, pyupgrade, pydocstyle, and bandit in a single tool — all configurable from pyproject.toml with a single pass over the codebase.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about the Python quality triad (ruff + mypy + pytest)?
cards.back: All three tools are independently authoritative, address distinct failure modes (style/bugs, types, behavior), and are all configured via pyproject.toml. Using all three is the professional baseline for new Python projects — no single tool substitutes for the others. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: When should you enable mypy --strict for a new project?
cards.back: From the first commit. Enabling --strict after a codebase grows requires annotating hundreds of existing functions. Starting strict means every added function is checked immediately. For existing codebases, use incremental adoption: annotate files as you touch them.

cards.front: What is the difference between ruff and mypy in the quality toolchain?
cards.back: Ruff is a linter and formatter — it checks style, formatting, unused imports, and docstring conventions. Mypy is a static type checker — it verifies that type annotations are correct and consistent across the codebase. Running both catches different failure modes.

cards.front: How do you configure ruff, mypy, and pytest in a single file?
cards.back: All three go in pyproject.toml under [tool.ruff], [tool.mypy], and [tool.pytest.ini_options] sections respectively. No separate .flake8, mypy.ini, or pytest.ini files are needed.

cards.front: Which ruff lint rule group enables pydocstyle (docstring enforcement)?
cards.back: The "D" rule group enables pydocstyle checks. Add select = ["D"] under [tool.ruff.lint] in pyproject.toml. This will flag missing or malformed docstrings as lint errors.

cards.front: What is the dual role of pyproject.toml in a Python project?
cards.back: First, it is the unified dev tool configuration hub — replacing setup.cfg, tox.ini, .flake8, and mypy.ini in a single file. Second, it is readable at runtime via tomllib (Python 3.11+) for scripts that need to consume project metadata or configuration.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about consolidating tool configuration into pyproject.toml?
cards.back: PEP 517/518 established pyproject.toml as the standard build configuration file. Ruff, mypy, and pytest all support it. The practical effect is eliminating 3-5 separate config files. Confidence: strong — all major tools support it; no evidence against consolidation.

cards.front: What is the difference between tomllib and a general TOML library like tomli?
cards.back: tomllib is read-only and is part of the Python 3.11+ standard library — it can parse TOML but cannot write it. tomli is the third-party backport for Python 3.8-3.10 with identical read-only API. Neither library can serialize Python objects to TOML.

cards.front: When is a plain INI file (configparser) more appropriate than TOML (tomllib)?
cards.back: When the config file is directly edited by end users who are not developers — INI syntax is simpler and more familiar to non-technical users. For developer-facing tool config, TOML is preferred for its expressiveness and standard library support.

cards.front: How do you read pyproject.toml at runtime from within a Python script?
cards.back: Use tomllib (Python 3.11+) or tomli (3.8–3.10) with a binary file open: `with open('pyproject.toml', 'rb') as f: data = tomllib.load(f)`. The 'rb' mode is required — tomllib only accepts binary file objects.

cards.front: What is the universal script skeleton in Python?
cards.back: A fixed structure: all domain logic in importable functions, a main() function that follows the parse → configure logging → validate → dispatch sequence, and an `if __name__ == '__main__': main()` guard at the bottom. The guard enables test imports without triggering execution.

cards.front: Why does the `if __name__ == '__main__'` guard matter for testability?
cards.back: Without the guard, `import myscript` in a test file executes the entire script. With the guard, tests can import and call individual functions in isolation — the guard block runs only when the file is executed directly, not when imported. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the required execution order inside main() and why?
cards.back: 1. Parse arguments (argparse) 2. Configure logging (basicConfig) 3. Validate inputs 4. Dispatch to domain functions. Logging must be configured before domain functions run so their output is correctly routed. Arguments must be parsed before logging so --log-level is available.

cards.front: How do you adapt the script skeleton for an asyncio script?
cards.back: Only the entry point changes: `if __name__ == '__main__': asyncio.run(main())`. The main() function becomes `async def main()`. The internal sequence (parse → log → validate → dispatch) is identical.

cards.front: What is the difference between putting code at module level vs. inside main()?
cards.back: Module-level code runs on every import, including in tests and other scripts. Code inside main() only runs when explicitly called. Domain logic at module level breaks test isolation and can cause hard-to-debug side effects when the module is imported.

cards.front: Where in a script should `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` appear?
cards.back: At module level, outside any function, at the top of the file after imports. This makes the logger available to all functions in the module and names it after the module for automatic source location in log output.

cards.front: What is the correct place to call logging.basicConfig() in a Python script?
cards.back: Exactly once, inside main(), after argument parsing so the --log-level flag can be applied. Never in imported library modules — they should only emit records, not configure handlers.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about using print() for diagnostic output in scripts?
cards.back: print() cannot be silenced, redirected to a file, or filtered by severity without editing source code. The Python logging HOWTO and Google Style Guide both recommend the logging module for all non-user-output messages. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the difference between logging setup in a script vs. a library?
cards.back: Scripts configure logging — they call basicConfig() in main() to set up handlers. Libraries must never configure logging — they install a NullHandler and emit records. If a library calls basicConfig(), it pollutes the log output of every application that imports it.

cards.front: Why should you use `logger.info('Value: %s', x)` instead of `logger.info(f'Value: {x}')`?
cards.back: The %-style format defers string formatting until the record is actually emitted. If the log level is WARNING, a DEBUG message using f-string formatting does the string work unnecessarily. The %-style skips formatting entirely for suppressed messages.

cards.front: What NullHandler pattern must a library follow to avoid polluting application logs?
cards.back: `logging.getLogger(__name__).addHandler(logging.NullHandler())` at module level in the library's __init__.py. This registers a handler that discards all records, preventing 'No handlers could be found' warnings without configuring output.

cards.front: What are the three mandatory elements of every public Python function?
cards.back: 1. A one-line summary docstring (PEP 257). 2. Type annotations on all parameters and the return value. 3. Raises section documenting non-obvious exceptions. Functions starting with _ (internal) are exempt from mandatory documentation.

cards.front: Why should you write a function's docstring before writing its implementation?
cards.back: Writing docstring first forces you to clarify the contract (what it accepts, returns, raises) before you commit to an implementation. This surfaces unclear requirements early and prevents documentation debt — the function is hardest to document after the implementation details are forgotten. Confidence: moderate (pragmatic consensus, not empirically measured).

cards.front: What is the difference between type annotations and docstrings — why do you need both?
cards.back: Type annotations are machine-verifiable contracts — mypy will catch callers passing wrong types at static analysis time. Docstrings are human-readable explanations of intent, edge cases, and behavior that no type checker can verify. Neither replaces the other.

cards.front: Which sections are required in a Google-style docstring for a non-trivial function?
cards.back: Args (with per-parameter descriptions and types if not using annotations), Returns (including empty/None case), and Raises (for any non-obvious exceptions). The one-line summary is always required. The extended description paragraph is optional.

cards.front: How do you enforce docstring presence automatically in a Python project?
cards.back: Add `select = ["D"]` under [tool.ruff.lint] in pyproject.toml. Ruff's pydocstyle integration will flag missing docstrings, wrong summary line format, and missing Args/Returns sections as lint errors in CI.

cards.front: What is TypedDict and when should you use it?
cards.back: TypedDict is a typing construct that defines dictionaries with known, typed keys. Use it when you have a dict whose shape is fixed — e.g., parsed config, API response objects, db rows. It gives mypy the ability to verify key names and value types rather than treating the dict as dict[str, Any].

cards.front: What changed in Python 3.10 for union type annotations?
cards.back: PEP 604 (Python 3.10) introduced the | operator for union types: `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, and `int | str` instead of `Union[int, str]`. The new syntax requires no imports from the typing module and is shorter and more readable. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: When would you use Protocol instead of ABC (abstract base class)?
cards.back: Use Protocol for structural typing — when you want to define an interface that any class matching the shape satisfies, without requiring inheritance. Protocol is ideal for duck-typed interfaces (e.g., Closeable, Readable) where you don't control the classes being typed.

cards.front: What is the difference between list[str] (Python 3.9+) and List[str] (pre-3.9)?
cards.back: Both are valid and equivalent at type-checking time. list[str] (lowercase) is the built-in generic form available since Python 3.9 — it requires no import. List[str] requires `from typing import List`. For new code on Python 3.9+, always prefer the built-in lowercase form.

cards.front: What does `Final` do and when is it useful?
cards.back: Final[T] tells mypy that a variable must not be reassigned after its initial value. Use it for module-level constants: `MAX_RETRIES: Final = 3`. Mypy will flag any code that tries to reassign the variable as a type error.

cards.front: What does mypy --strict enable beyond the default checks?
cards.back: Enables: disallow_untyped_defs (all functions must be annotated), disallow_any_generics (no bare List or Dict), warn_return_any, warn_unused_ignores, strict_equality, and several others. Recommended for new projects from day one.

cards.front: What does pathlib.Path replace in older Python code?
cards.back: pathlib.Path replaces os.path (join, basename, dirname, exists, isdir), os.getcwd(), os.makedirs(), and open() for simple text reads/writes. It makes paths first-class objects with methods: .read_text(), .write_text(), .glob(), .parent, .stem, and the / operator for joining.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about using context managers for resource management?
cards.back: The `with` statement guarantees __exit__ runs even when exceptions occur. Python's documentation, Google Style Guide, and PEP 343 all mandate the with-statement for files, connections, and any closeable resource. Without it, exceptions before .close() leak the resource. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the difference between tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() and tempfile.mktemp()?
cards.back: TemporaryDirectory() is a context manager that creates a directory, yields its path, and guarantees deletion on __exit__. mktemp() only returns a name — the caller must create and delete the directory manually. mktemp() also has a race condition: another process can claim the name between mktemp() and the actual creation.

cards.front: How do you create /reports/2026/output.txt with pathlib, creating all parent directories?
cards.back: `path = Path('/reports/2026/output.txt'); path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True); path.write_text(content, encoding='utf-8')`. The `parents=True` creates the full directory chain. `exist_ok=True` prevents an error if the directory already exists.

cards.front: Which pathlib method efficiently reads all .json files in a directory tree?
cards.back: `path.rglob('*.json')` for recursive search, or `path.glob('*.json')` for the immediate directory only. Both return generators of Path objects. Prefer `rglob` when the directory structure has unknown depth.

cards.front: When should you build a custom context manager with @contextmanager?
cards.back: When you need setup/teardown semantics for a resource that doesn't already implement __enter__/__exit__ — e.g., database transactions, temporary env var overrides, mock state. Use contextlib.contextmanager and yield once inside a try/finally block.

cards.front: What is the four-tier configuration priority stack for Python scripts?
cards.back: From lowest to highest priority: 1. Hardcoded defaults (in code). 2. Config files (TOML/INI). 3. Environment variables. 4. CLI flags. Apply them in this order — later sources overwrite earlier ones — so the user's explicit flags always win.

cards.front: Where should secrets (API keys, passwords) live in a Python script's configuration?
cards.back: Environment variables only — never in config files, source code, or version control. Even a .env file is a git add accident away from exposure. Read secrets with `os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY')` and fail clearly if absent rather than silently using a None value. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the difference between tomllib and configparser for runtime config?
cards.back: tomllib (TOML) is read-only, supports nested structures and typed values (int, bool, datetime), and is best for developer-facing config. configparser (INI) supports read and write, flat key-value sections, and string interpolation — better for end-user editable config files.

cards.front: How do you implement the four-tier config stack so CLI flags always win?
cards.back: Build a dict by applying sources from lowest to highest priority using dict.update(): start with defaults → update with file config → update with env vars → update with CLI args (skipping args that are None/unset). Each layer overwrites the previous, so CLI is last and always wins.

cards.front: What should a script do when a required environment variable secret is missing?
cards.back: Log a warning and degrade gracefully if the feature is optional, or raise a clear error with an explanatory message (e.g., `raise EnvironmentError('APP_SECRET not set. Export this variable before running.')`) for mandatory secrets. Never silently use None or an empty string.

cards.front: Why must tomllib.load() receive a binary file object?
cards.back: The TOML spec requires UTF-8 encoding, and tomllib validates encoding at the binary level before decoding. Passing a text-mode file object (open(path, 'r')) raises a TypeError. Always use `open(path, 'rb')` or `Path.open('rb')` when loading TOML.

cards.front: What is the trust-level rule for choosing a serialization format?
cards.back: Format choice is a security decision based on the source of the data: JSON/CSV for any input (safe to deserialize from untrusted sources), TOML/INI for trusted developer config, pickle for internal-only trusted process communication. Never use pickle for network data or user-supplied files.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about the security risk of pickle?
cards.back: The Python pickle documentation states explicitly: 'Never unpickle data that could have come from an untrusted source.' Pickle deserialization executes arbitrary Python code — a crafted pickle payload is a remote code execution vector. This is not a theoretical risk. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the difference between json.loads() and pickle.loads() when given malicious input?
cards.back: json.loads() on malicious input raises json.JSONDecodeError — the worst outcome is a parsing exception. pickle.loads() on malicious input executes the attacker's arbitrary Python code — the worst outcome is full system compromise. JSON is safe to use on untrusted data; pickle is not.

cards.front: Which format should you use for data received from an HTTP API endpoint?
cards.back: JSON. It is the de facto standard for HTTP APIs, is safe to deserialize from untrusted sources (worst case: JSONDecodeError), and is part of the Python standard library. Never use pickle for network-received data under any circumstances.

cards.front: How should you handle a security linter warning for a deliberate, safe pickle.load() call?
cards.back: Suppress it on the specific line with a comment explaining why it is safe: `pickle.load(f)  # noqa: S301 — internal cache written by this process only`. Never disable the security rule project-wide — that would suppress the check on future unsafe uses.

cards.front: What is the GIL, and how does it affect Python concurrency choice?
cards.back: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. It is released during I/O waits, so threads (and asyncio) can parallelize I/O-bound work. For CPU-bound work, the GIL prevents true parallelism — use ProcessPoolExecutor to spawn separate interpreter processes that each have their own GIL.

cards.front: What does the evidence say about using asyncio for CPU-bound work?
cards.back: asyncio is cooperative: tasks yield only during await expressions. A CPU-bound coroutine that never awaits will block the entire event loop, starving all other coroutines. The asyncio docs, concurrent.futures docs, and Real Python all agree: asyncio is for I/O-bound work; ProcessPoolExecutor is for CPU-bound work. Confidence: strong.

cards.front: What is the difference between ThreadPoolExecutor and ProcessPoolExecutor?
cards.back: ThreadPoolExecutor uses OS threads — they share memory and the GIL, so they parallelize I/O-bound work but not CPU-bound work. ProcessPoolExecutor spawns separate processes — each has its own GIL and memory, allowing true CPU parallelism but with higher overhead and no shared memory.

cards.front: What is asyncio.to_thread() and when should you use it?
cards.back: asyncio.to_thread(fn, *args) runs a synchronous blocking function in a thread pool without blocking the event loop. Use it when you must call a sync library (e.g., file I/O, legacy SDK) from an async function. It is the clean alternative to run_in_executor with the default executor.

cards.front: What is asyncio.TaskGroup and what problem does it solve over asyncio.gather()?
cards.back: TaskGroup (Python 3.11+) provides structured concurrency: if any task in the group raises an exception, all remaining tasks are automatically cancelled and the exception propagates. asyncio.gather() by default swallows exceptions from other tasks unless return_exceptions=False. TaskGroup is the preferred pattern for fan-out concurrent work.

cards.front: How do you offload a CPU-bound function to a process pool from async code?
cards.back: `loop = asyncio.get_event_loop(); result = await loop.run_in_executor(pool, fn, arg)` where pool is a ProcessPoolExecutor. In Python 3.9+, you can also use the default executor for threads: `await asyncio.to_thread(fn, arg)` (threads only — for processes, run_in_executor with an explicit ProcessPoolExecutor is required).

cards.front: What is the 'one question' that determines which concurrency model to use?
cards.back: Is the program waiting or computing? Waiting (network, disk, sleep) → asyncio or threads (I/O-bound). Computing (math, parsing, compression) → ProcessPoolExecutor (CPU-bound). This question eliminates most wrong concurrency choices before writing any code.

cards.front: What is graceful shutdown in the context of a Python script?
cards.back: Graceful shutdown means: receiving a termination signal (SIGTERM/SIGINT), stopping new work, allowing in-flight operations to complete or cleanly cancel, running all cleanup code (flush buffers, close connections, commit transactions), then exiting with a clean return code.

cards.front: Why must asyncio.CancelledError always be re-raised after catching it?
cards.back: CancelledError is the mechanism by which structured concurrency propagates cancellation up the call chain. If you catch and swallow it, the event loop believes the coroutine finished successfully, breaks cooperative cancellation, and can leave TaskGroup and other callers waiting indefinitely. Always: except CancelledError: cleanup(); raise.

cards.front: How do you register a SIGTERM handler that triggers a clean asyncio shutdown?
cards.back: Use `loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, callback)` inside main(). The callback sets an asyncio.Event. The main coroutine awaits the event, then cancels running tasks. Use this instead of signal.signal() so the handler interacts correctly with the event loop.

cards.front: What is the difference between SIGTERM handling on Linux/macOS vs. Windows?
cards.back: On Linux/macOS, SIGTERM is a real OS signal — processes can register handlers for it. `loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM, ...)` works correctly. On Windows, SIGTERM is not a real signal — it is emulated and `loop.add_signal_handler()` is not available. Windows Python processes should handle only SIGINT (Ctrl+C), and rely on platform process management for termination.

cards.front: How does ProcessPoolExecutor guarantee cleanup when used as a context manager?
cards.back: `with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:` calls pool.shutdown(wait=True) on __exit__. This waits for all currently queued tasks to complete before the with-block exits. Use the context manager form (not pool.shutdown() manually) to ensure cleanup even if an exception occurs.

cards.front: Where should cleanup code (DB commit, file flush, connection close) go in an async task?
cards.back: In the `finally` block of the task's main try/except block. The `finally` block runs whether the coroutine completes normally, raises an exception, or receives CancelledError. This guarantees cleanup regardless of the shutdown path.

cards.front: What did PEP 484 say about type annotations being mandatory?
cards.back: PEP 484 (2015) explicitly states: 'Python will remain a dynamically typed language, and the authors have no desire to ever make type hints mandatory or even ubiquitous.' This describes language design policy — not professional coding practice. Confidence: strong (directly quoted from PEP 484).

cards.front: What is the difference between 'language policy' (PEP 484) and 'team practice' (Google Style Guide) on annotations?
cards.back: PEP 484 describes what Python allows — annotations are optional by language design. Google Style Guide describes what a professional team should require — annotate all new public code. These operate at different levels of authority and are not contradictory. For production code, follow team practice.

cards.front: When a student cites PEP 484's 'never mandatory' to avoid type annotations, what is the correct teaching response?
cards.back: Distinguish language policy from team standards. PEP 484 says the language won't require them — not that professionals shouldn't use them. For new projects, full annotation with --strict is the professional default. For existing codebases, annotate files as you touch them. PEP 484 does not resolve team annotation requirements.

cards.front: What four topics are explicitly out of scope for this course?
cards.back: G010: Packaging and distribution (pyproject.toml [project.scripts], wheels, PyPI). G011: Dependency management (uv, Poetry, pip-compile, virtual environments). G012: Pre-commit hooks (the pre-commit framework). G013: Dataclasses and attrs (typed data container classes beyond TypedDict).

cards.front: Where should a learner go after this course to learn Python dependency management?
cards.back: The uv documentation (docs.astral.sh/uv) for the fastest modern dependency manager with virtual environment management. Alternatively, pip-tools (pip-tools.readthedocs.io) for lock-file generation with pip. Both tools are production-ready and align with the ruff/pyproject.toml ecosystem used in this course.

cards.front: What is the minimum pre-commit configuration to enforce the quality toolchain from this course?
cards.back: Install ruff-pre-commit (hook id: ruff and ruff-format) from astral-sh, and mirrors-mypy (hook id: mypy). These run ruff lint, ruff format, and mypy on every `git commit`. The course's pyproject.toml configs are picked up automatically.

questions.question: A colleague proposes adding separate .flake8, mypy.ini, and pytest.ini files to a new Python project. What is the strongest counter-argument?

options.A: Those file formats are deprecated and no longer supported by the tools
options.B: All three tools support [tool.*] sections in pyproject.toml, so the project can use a single authoritative config file
options.C: It is impossible to run ruff, mypy, and pytest from the same project without conflicts
options.D: pyproject.toml is only for packaging and cannot configure dev tools

questions.explanation: ruff, mypy, and pytest all support pyproject.toml configuration under [tool.ruff], [tool.mypy], and [tool.pytest.ini_options] respectively. Separate config files still work but add unnecessary fragmentation. A, C, and D are factually incorrect.

questions.question: A script codebase has ruff configured but not mypy. A function that returns str | None is called later in the same file with .upper() called on the result without a None check. What does ruff catch that mypy would catch, and vice versa?

options.A: Ruff would catch the missing None check; mypy would catch the style issue
options.B: Ruff would catch style and formatting issues; mypy would catch the missing None check as a type error
options.C: Both tools would flag the missing None check equally
options.D: Neither tool would flag the missing None check — it would only be caught at runtime

questions.explanation: Ruff is a linter/formatter — it checks style, formatting, and some static code issues but does not perform type inference. Mypy is a static type checker — it would flag `result.upper()` as a potential AttributeError because result could be None. This illustrates why both tools are necessary.

questions.question: You are starting a new project and decide to enable mypy --strict from the first commit rather than adding it later. What is the primary benefit of this timing?

options.A: mypy --strict is only compatible with projects under 1000 lines of code
options.B: Enabling --strict at the start means every new function is checked immediately; retrofitting it to a large codebase requires annotating hundreds of existing functions at once
options.C: mypy --strict automatically generates type annotations for unannotated code
options.D: CI pipelines reject projects that add mypy --strict after the initial commit

questions.explanation: The cost of adopting mypy --strict scales with codebase size. Starting from the first commit means the 'annotation debt' is always zero — every added function must be annotated before the check passes. A, C, and D are incorrect.

questions.question: A Python script needs to read its own pyproject.toml at runtime to display the project version. Which approach is correct?

options.A: Use json.load() after renaming the file to .json
options.B: Open the file in text mode and use tomllib.load()
options.C: Open the file in binary mode ('rb') and use tomllib.load()
options.D: tomllib cannot read pyproject.toml at runtime — use configparser instead

questions.explanation: tomllib.load() requires a binary file object — it validates UTF-8 encoding at the binary level. Opening in text mode ('r') raises a TypeError. D is wrong because tomllib is specifically designed for TOML files. A is incorrect because TOML and JSON are different formats.

questions.question: Your team is releasing a Python library that will be imported into other applications. You want users to be able to silence all logging from your library. What should your library's __init__.py do?

options.A: Call logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARNING) to set a default quiet level
options.B: Add a NullHandler to the package logger: logging.getLogger(__name__).addHandler(logging.NullHandler())
options.C: Set logging.disable(logging.CRITICAL) to suppress all messages by default
options.D: Configure a FileHandler so library logs go to a separate file

questions.explanation: The NullHandler pattern is the correct library pattern — it discards records by default but allows the importing application to configure its own handlers for the library's logger. A, C, and D all configure logging from within the library, which overrides the importing application's logging setup.

questions.question: A team member argues that because ruff replaces five tools, adding mypy is redundant. How would you respond?

options.A: Agree — ruff's type-checking rules cover the same ground as mypy
options.B: Disagree — ruff's 'T' rules check type annotation syntax, but ruff does not perform cross-file type inference; mypy verifies that annotations are semantically correct
options.C: Disagree — ruff is only a formatter and has no lint functionality
options.D: Agree — running both tools causes configuration conflicts in pyproject.toml

questions.explanation: Ruff can check annotation syntax and flag missing annotations, but it does not perform type inference — it cannot verify that a function receiving str | None is called safely. Mypy performs full static type checking including cross-file inference. They serve different purposes.

questions.question: Explain the dual role of pyproject.toml in a Python project and describe one limitation of tomllib that is relevant to its runtime use.

required_points: Must identify pyproject.toml as dev tool config hub (replaces separate mypy.ini, .flake8, etc.)
Must identify pyproject.toml as readable at runtime via tomllib for project metadata/config
Must note that tomllib is read-only (cannot write/serialize TOML)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May phrase the dual role as 'build configuration' and 'tool configuration'. May mention tomli backport for pre-3.11.
answer_key.full_credit: Identifies both roles clearly and notes tomllib's read-only limitation with an explanation of why it matters.
answer_key.partial_credit: Identifies only one role, or mentions the limitation without explaining the consequence.

questions.question: Describe a scenario where running ruff without mypy would fail to catch a critical bug, and explain why mypy would catch it.

required_points: Must describe a type-related scenario: e.g., calling a method on a potentially-None value, passing wrong type to a function, returning wrong type
Must explain that ruff does not perform type inference across the codebase
Must explain that mypy traces the type through the call chain and flags the mismatch
